front: Composer: dominant figure in French grand opera; fused German orchestration, Italian melody, and French drama; born Jakob Liebmann Beer in Berlin; works include Les Huguenots and Robert le Diable
Composer: targeted by Wagner's anti-Semitic Das Judenthum in der Musik and Opera and Drama; ironically was one of Wagner's early supporters; Wagner's Rienzi was called 'Meyerbeer's best opera'
Composer: Il crociato in Egitto (his final Italian opera) was likely the last opera to feature a castrato and the last to call for keyboard accompaniment in recitatives
Opera: set during the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre; Protestant tenor Raoul and Catholic Valentine are killed by her father Saint-Bris; overture quotes Luther's 'Ein feste Burg'
Opera: servant Marcel sings the battle song 'Piff, paff' imitating cannons; Act IV features the 'Blessing of the Daggers,' praised by Berlioz in three analytical articles
Opera: calls for a viola d'amore in the aria 'Plus blanc que la blanche hermine'; often only performed through Act IV

Opera: Act III 'Ballet of the Nuns' — villain Bertram summons dead nuns, Abbess Helena (Marie Taglioni) leads them in dance; exploited new gas lighting at the Paris Opéra; credited with introducing the ballet tutu
Opera: ends with cello accompanying a character's hallucination of her lover in a swan-drawn chariot; Berlioz praised its use of gongs and bassoon

Opera: about John of Leyden; Anabaptists proclaim him a prophet to seize Münster; Act III skating ballet in a Westphalia forest; ends with Berthe setting fire to the palace, causing an explosion that kills everyone
Opera: final opera, originally titled Vasco da Gama; explorer sings 'O Paradis!' on Selika's island; Selika dies inhaling manchineel tree fumes; slave Nelusko sings the 'Ballad of Adamastor'
